critic_raw:
This multimodal neural network for post-operative complication prediction has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. COMPOSITE OUTCOME LABEL (SIX COMPLICATION TYPES) TREATS HETEROGENEOUS OUTCOMES AS EQUIVALENT. The positive label encompasses surgical site infection, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, and return to OR within 90 days -- six clinically distinct outcomes with different mechanisms, risk factors, and clinical implications. Aggregating them into a single binary label assumes that the model learns a unified risk factor profile that predicts all these events equally. In practice, risk factors for SSI (surgical technique, wound class) differ substantially from risk factors for VTE (immobility, clotting disorders) or AKI (baseline renal function, contrast exposure). A model trained on the composite label may learn risk factors for the most common complication in the training set while appearing to predict the aggregate outcome well.

2. STRATIFICATION BY SURGEON AND COMPLICATION RATE QUARTILE WITHIN TEMPORAL WINDOWS INTRODUCES SURGEON IDENTITY AS AN IMPLICIT FEATURE. The design stratifies by surgeon within each temporal window. Surgeons are balanced across train, validation, and test by this stratification. But surgeon identity is also included as a fixed effect in the logistic regression baseline. If surgeons differ substantially in complication rates and surgeon identity appears as a fixed effect, the model has learned surgeon-specific intercepts. On the test set, if surgeons are balanced, the fixed-effect advantage is preserved. But in deployment, new surgeons or surgeons with few records in the training set will have poorly estimated fixed effects. The stratification by surgeon creates an artificially favorable evaluation scenario relative to deployment.

3. FROZEN PRETRAINED CLINICAL LANGUAGE MODEL FOR NOTE ENCODING IS NOT VALIDATED ON THIS INSTITUTION'S NOTE STYLE. The design uses SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC as the note encoder, frozen during training. MIMIC contains notes from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. If the institution in this experiment has different note conventions, abbreviation styles, or template structures, the frozen encoder may produce systematically different representations for semantically equivalent clinical content. The design does not validate that the note embeddings from the frozen encoder are meaningful in the context of this institution's operative reports.

4. PRIMARY METRIC (SENSITIVITY AT 85% SPECIFICITY) IS VALID-SET OPTIMIZED THEN TEST-SET EVALUATED, BUT THRESHOLD CHOICE IS ARBITRARY. The validation set is used to select the model and, implicitly, to identify the operating point achieving 85% specificity. The 85% specificity threshold is presented as clinically motivated but is not referenced to any established clinical standard or cost-benefit analysis. On the test set, the model is evaluated at the threshold selected to achieve 85% specificity on validation -- but validation-set specificity does not guarantee test-set specificity at the same threshold if the test distribution differs. Test-set sensitivity is thus evaluated at a threshold that may not achieve 85% specificity on the test set.

5. 90-DAY OUTCOME WINDOW CREATES TEMPORAL LEAKAGE AT SPLIT BOUNDARIES. The outcome is defined as any complication within 90 days post-surgery. For surgeries occurring in the last 3 months of the training window (months 34-36), the 90-day outcome window extends into the validation period (months 37-39). If records from the validation period are used to construct training labels, there is label leakage. Conversely, if 90-day windows that extend beyond the training period are excluded or right-censored, the training set systematically underrepresents recent surgeries, biasing the model toward older surgical practices.
all_issues_raised: Composite binary complication label aggregates six mechanistically heterogeneous outcomes, training a unified model on incompatible risk profiles
Surgeon stratification within splits and surgeon fixed effects create artificially favorable evaluation for surgeons seen in training
Frozen pretrained MIMIC-based note encoder may not generalize to the study institution's note conventions
Sensitivity at 85% specificity threshold chosen on validation may not achieve 85% specificity on the test set
90-day outcome window creates label leakage for surgeries near training-to-validation split boundary
Composite complication endpoint conflates outcomes of vastly different clinical severity; model winning on composite may be predicting only readmission
Surgeon fixed effects learned in training may not transfer to test period if surgeon practice changes; temporal mismatch in surgeon-level effects unaddressed
Sensitivity at 85% specificity threshold set on validation distribution; threshold may not achieve 85% specificity on test set
MIMIC-trained clinical language model embeddings not validated for orthopedic surgery operative note vocabulary
Case-control balancing ratio not specified; arbitrary balancing inflates calibration error relative to true clinical prevalence
Composite complication label collapses six mechanistically distinct outcomes, masking where neural network advantage actually originates
Artificial class balancing creates train/test distribution mismatch and produces miscalibrated models when applied to naturally imbalanced test data
Sensitivity at 85% specificity threshold derived from validation set may not correspond to 85% specificity on a temporally shifted test set
Frozen BioBERT/SciBERT embeddings from general biomedical pretraining are mismatched to orthopedic surgical note language
Surgeon identifier treated as fixed effect in logistic regression vs. learned embedding in neural network creates asymmetric information capture
